sub_topics.title: Exploration of Sounds
competences: Critical Thinking: Make simple logical conclusions
Creativity and Innovation: Use imagination and creativity to solve problems

instructional_blocks.hook: Ask learners: 'What sounds can you hear right now in the classroom or outside?' Let them mention and mimic what they hear.
teacher_steps: Show real cleaning items or pictures of sound sources
Guide learners to listen to sounds around them
Divide learners into small groups
Demonstrate how to create sounds using body parts and objects
learner_steps: Listen to the sounds around them
Identify the sounds they heard and try to imitate each sound
Use items (sticks, bottles) or their bodies (clapping, whistling) to produce a unique sound
Present their sounds to the class

sub_topics.short_notes: Exploring sounds means discovering and learning how different sounds are made, what causes them, and how they can be used to express ideas. It helps children sharpen their listening skills and encourages creative thinking. Sound is a form of energy produced by vibrating objects. Sounds are part of our everyday life, and they can be natural (like birds singing or wind blowing) or artificial (like car horns or bells). By exploring and experimenting with sounds, learners develop creativity, listening skills, and the ability to express themselves.

vocabulary.definition: A form of energy produced by vibrating objects
To investigate or discover something new
Sounds from nature like animals, wind, or rain
Man-made sounds like machines, bells, or cars

criteria: Observe whether learners are actively creating and imitating sounds (clapping, tapping, hissing)
Assess the ability to imitate and express sounds through voice or body
Assess the ability to listen attentively and observe surroundings to identify various sounds
Assess the ability to use body parts (hands, mouth, fingers) to produce different sounds

sub_topics.title: Practicing Simple Plays
competences: Critical Thinking: Solve basic problems logically
Creativity and Innovation: Use imagination and creativity to solve problems
Collaboration: Participate in and express themselves through creative arts
Emotional Intelligence: Show sympathy and empathy, recognize personal emotions and that of others, communicate with others

instructional_blocks.hook: Today, you get to become actors! Imagine you are the teacher, your friend is a doctor, and someone else is a shopkeeper. How would you act? Let's have fun pretending to be different people!
teacher_steps: Divide learners into small groups
Give each group a different theme to act out (a market scene, classroom, a family dinner, hospital visit)
Let them practice with your help, using simple props and imagination
At the end, each group performs their drama in front of the class
learner_steps: Take turns acting as other people like a doctor, parent, or shopkeeper
Use appropriate vocabulary and tone during role play
Express feelings and emotions through drama
Work together in groups to create a scene

sub_topics.short_notes: Drama is a fun and creative way of telling stories by acting, talking, and using movement. It helps learners to express emotions, solve problems, and understand different ideas through play. Drama activities can help learners become more confident, imaginative, and better at working with others. Drama allows learners to express themselves by pretending to be other people or things. It helps learners use their imagination and builds their communication and problem-solving skills.

vocabulary.definition: A way of telling stories using acting, talking, and movement
Pretending to be someone else in a fun and playful way to learn and understand their actions or feelings

criteria: Assess the use of appropriate vocabulary and tone during role play
Assess the ability to demonstrate creative thinking when acting out a theme or scene
Assess willing to express feelings and emotions through drama
Learner applies understanding of daily life experiences in dramatizing real-life situations
Assess coordination and spatial awareness during performance

sub_topics.title: Customer Service
competences: Emotional Intelligence: handle guests appropriately
Communication: Use appropriate language (verbal and non-verbal) in different situations
Digital Literacy: Write, read and send text messages, make and answer a phone call

instructional_blocks.hook: Imagine you are visiting a friend's house. How would you feel if they greeted you with a smile and made you feel happy? And how about if their house was nice and clean? You would probably feel really good, right? Today, we will learn how to be kind and helpful to guests and how to keep rooms clean and tidy so everyone feels happy and comfortable!
teacher_steps: Guide learners to practice greeting each other in pairs through a song
Show learners how to greet in different ways (handshake, hugging)
Demonstrate role-playing scenarios (welcoming a new learner, helping a friend locate a tuck-shop, inviting a new friend to play)
Provide feedback on the proper way of greeting and welcoming
Explain how to answer a phone call politely
learner_steps: Practice greeting each other in pairs through a song
Show other ways of greeting (handshake, hugging)
Role-play welcoming scenarios
Practice answering phone calls politely using a toy phone

sub_topics.short_notes: Hospitality means being kind, helpful and welcoming to visitors or guests. When we practice customer service skills, we make people feel happy and welcome. This is important in places like homes, schools, restaurants, and hotels. Customer service is the support and assistance provided to customers by a business or organization before, during and after the purchase or interaction. It involves understanding customer needs, resolving issues, and providing solutions to ensure customer satisfaction and loyalty.

vocabulary.definition: Being kind and nice to people
Receiving and greeting people with a smile and kind words
Assisting others when they need something
A person who visits
showing good manners, respect and consideration towards others
A guest who uses a service such as staying at a hotel, eating at a restaurant
Support and help provided by a company or organization to its customers before, during and after a purchase or interaction

criteria: Ability to speak clearly and effectively while greeting and welcoming others
Ability to answer the phone politely and respond appropriately in a conversation
Understanding and application of polite greetings and response

sub_topics.title: Cleaning
competences: Emotional Intelligence: handle guests appropriately
Communication: Use appropriate language (verbal and non-verbal) in different situations

teacher_steps: Show real cleaning items (broom, cloth, detergent, water, dustpan)
Display charts with pictures of cleaning items
Guide learners to practice cleaning with different items
Demonstrate sweeping, wiping, and using detergent
Observe and probe learners during the activities
Give feedback and correct mistakes
learner_steps: Name cleaning items and describe their use
Take turns sweeping, wiping, and using detergent
Clean different areas like the table, floor, and chair
Pick up litter and dispose of it correctly
Arrange furniture neatly after cleaning

sub_topics.short_notes: Cleaning is important to keep our rooms neat and safe. We use different items like brooms, cloths, and detergents to remove dirt and make rooms look nice. Cleaning helps keep rooms neat, safe, and healthy. It involves using tools such as brooms, cloths, detergents, and dustpans to remove dirt. Sweeping, wiping, and washing help make spaces look clean, tidy, and pleasant.

criteria: Assess understanding and knowledge of various cleaning tools and their uses
Assess application of correct cleaning techniques using different tools (broom, mop, dustpan, cloth)
Ability to demonstrate use of cleaning tools effectively and safely

sub_topics.title: ICT Devices
competences: Digital literacy: Use a computer or digital device basics with less difficulty, write, read, and send text messages, make and answer a phone call
Communication: Express oneself using different media and symbols
Critical thinking: Organize things according to their attributes, manipulate different objects

instructional_blocks.hook: Show learners real or pictures of samples of different ICT devices (Smart phone, TV, Computer). Ask learners if they have seen or used any of such devices.
teacher_steps: Show real ICT devices or pictures
Guide learners to explore devices
Demonstrate how to turn devices on/off
Explain the importance of turning off devices
Let learners practice with supervision
learner_steps: Explore real ICT devices or pictures
Match ICT devices with their correct names
Demonstrate switching on/off of ICT devices
Role-play making phone calls and video calls

sub_topics.short_notes: In this topic, learners will be introduced to basic ICT devices that they encounter in real life situations. They will learn to identify, use, and handle devices such as desktop computers, laptops, smart phones, televisions, and radios. This topic helps learners develop familiarity with technology, enhances their curiosity, and builds foundational digital literacy skills. Understanding these devices will help them become familiar with technology and its uses in real life situations.

vocabulary.definition: Machines used for communication, entertainment, and work (computers, smartphones, televisions, and radios)
A large computer placed on a table, used for work and study
A portable computer that can be used anywhere
Small computers that fit in your hand and can make calls, take pictures, and browse the internet
A machine that prints words and pictures onto paper
A screen used to watch videos and shows
A device used to listen to music, news, and other programs

criteria: Assess the ability to identify and name different ICT devices (desktop, laptop, smartphone, television, printer)
Assess the ability to operate ICT devices, specifically turning them on and off correctly
Assess the ability to apply their understanding of ICT devices to complete tasks, such as switching devices on/off and simulating digital communication scenarios

sub_topics.title: Computer Educational Games
competences: Digital literacy: Use a computer or digital device basics with less difficulty
Communication: Express oneself using different media and symbols
Critical thinking: Organise things according to their attributes, manipulate different objects

teacher_steps: Demonstrate how to play educational games
Let learners try simple actions like clicking or tapping
Provide instructions on following game rules
Give feedback on voice tone and politeness
Explain important reminders for playing games
learner_steps: Try simple actions like clicking or tapping to interact with computer games
Explore different educational games
Follow game instructions
Take turns sharing the computer or tablet with friends
Play for a short time and rest their eyes

sub_topics.short_notes: In this topic, learners will be introduced to basic computer software concepts through interactive educational games. They will explore how games can be used as learning tools to enhance problem-solving, creativity, and digital literacy. By engaging with computer educational games, learners will develop hand-eye coordination, critical thinking, and decision-making skills. This subtopic introduces learners to computer educational games, teaching them how to play simple games that develop their problem-solving and cognitive skills.

vocabulary.definition: Special programs on a computer that help us do things like play games, write stories, or search for information
A fun game on the computer or tablet that helps us learn new things, like math or reading
A program on a phone or computer that helps us do specific things, like taking pictures or playing music
Things we use to control the computer. The mouse moves the pointer, and the keyboard helps us type words
A screen that lets us touch it with our fingers to do things, like tap on pictures or scroll through pages

criteria: Assess ability to interact with computer games by performing basic actions like clicking, tapping, or using a mouse or touchscreen
Assess ability to follow game instructions, listening carefully and performing tasks as directed by the game
Assess collaborative behaviour by sharing the computer or tablet with peers and taking turns during game time

sub_topics.title: Fitness and Health
competences: Collaboration: Participate in and express themselves through creative arts
Creativity and Innovation: Apply a set of skills that are appropriate for the creative process

instructional_blocks.hook: Begin with a fun song or chant that includes actions such as running in place, jumping, and stretching. Example: 'Head, shoulders, knees, and toes' or 'If you're happy and you know it, jump up high!'
teacher_steps: Lead warm-up activities (marching, arm swings, deep breathing)
Demonstrate running relay, jumping line game, climbing and crawling activities
Guide slider activity
Lead breathing practice
Guide cool-down activities (slow walking, gentle stretching, final deep breathing)
Keep activities short and fun to maintain attention
Model each activity and guide learners through safe usage of equipment
Encourage all learners
learner_steps: Perform marching in place, arm swings, deep breathing exercises
Run relay from one cone to another and return
Jump back and forth over a rope or line on the ground
Climb ladders or stairs and crawl under a rope
Slide down gently and land on a mat
Stand, close eyes, breathe in deeply through the nose and out through the mouth
Perform slow walking, gentle stretching, final deep breathing

sub_topics.short_notes: This topic helps learners understand the importance of health and fitness for their bodies and minds. Health means being free from illness and feeling well, while fitness is having the strength and energy to carry out daily activities. Through fun physical exercises such as running, jumping, climbing, and stretching, learners will improve their strength, balance, flexibility, and coordination. These activities not only strengthen the body but also build confidence, creativity, and teamwork. Fitness and health are important for keeping our bodies strong and active. This topic is key in helping learners take part in fun physical activities that improve their body strength, movement, and health through practicing jumping, running, climbing, and deep breathing while using ropes, ladders, and other equipment.

vocabulary.definition: Being strong, healthy, and able to do physical activities easily
A state of being well in body and mind, and free from illness
Moving upward using hands and feet, like going up a ladder or tree
Pushing your body off the ground into the air using your legs
Moving quickly on your feet, faster than walking
Slowly taking air in through the nose and out through the mouth to help the body relax
Tools or things used to do a certain activity, like balls, ropes, or mats for exercise

criteria: Assess demonstration of basic motor skills such as running, jumping, climbing, crawling, and sliding in a controlled manner
Assess ability to perform deep breathing exercises as part of the warm-up, main activities, and cool-down sessions
Assess awareness of safe practices during physical activities by following instructions and using equipment appropriately, especially during activities like climbing and sliding
Assess cooperation and teamwork during group activities such as the running relay, learning to work together and take turns

sub_topics.title: Educational Gymnastics
competences: Collaboration: Participate in and express themselves through creative arts
Creativity and Innovation: Apply a set of skills that are appropriate for the creative process

teacher_steps: Lead warm-up activities (stretching arms, legs, back, marching in place, deep breathing)
Demonstrate and assist learners in forward roll
Guide frog jumps
Demonstrate cartwheel (optional) with teacher support
Guide arm rolls, leg rolls, squatting, and balancing activities
Lead cool-down activities (gentle stretching, slow breathing, sitting quietly)
Demonstrate each movement slowly and clearly
Always supervise closely during rolling or balancing activities
Praise effort, not perfection
learner_steps: Perform stretching arms, legs, back, marching in place, deep breathing
Roll forward on a mat
Squat and jump forward like a frog
Attempt cartwheel with support (optional)
Circle arms in large movements
Sit down and roll legs back and forth
Practice squat and stand several times
Stand on one foot for 5-10 seconds, try balancing on a small beam or line
Perform gentle stretching, slow breathing, sit quietly

sub_topics.short_notes: Gymnastics is a fun way to make our bodies strong and flexible. Learners will perform simple tumbling activities such as forward rolls and frog jumps, as well as stunt movements like squatting, balancing, and arm and leg rolling. These activities improve coordination and help learners become more aware of how their bodies move. This topic is key in helping learners explore educational gymnastics through tumbling and stunt activities by practicing movements that help build body strength, flexibility, and balance. Also by learning how to roll, jump, and balance safely.

criteria: Assess the ability to perform tumbling activities (frog jump, forward roll)
Demonstrate stunt activities (squatting, arm rolling, balancing)
Follow safety rules and participate confidently
Assess understanding of different tumbling activities and stunts

sub_topics.title: Cultures and Landmarks
competences: Citizenship: Appreciate and respect different cultures and landmarks in their immediate environment
Emotional Intelligence: Develop curiosity and awareness of cultural diversity
Problem solving: Learn to locate landmarks using cardinal points
Communication: Use appropriate language (verbal and non-verbal) in different situations, express oneself using different media and symbols

instructional_blocks.hook: Display images or videos of different cultural practices and landmarks. Sing a song or recite a poem about different cultures, or use a simple game where learners give directions to common landmarks.
teacher_steps: Show pictures of people in different traditional attire
Lead group discussion about what learners notice in pictures
Show pictures of people in different traditional clothes or eating different foods
Tell or play a short cultural story (folk tales)
Invite guest speaker to share cultural tale or song (optional)
Ask questions about the story
Bring traditional clothes for learners to try on
Play traditional songs for dance and clapping games
Show pictures of different traditional dishes
Guide craft or drawing activity
learner_steps: Match cultural names to pictures
Discuss what they notice in pictures
Listen to or retell simple stories from different cultural groups
Answer questions about stories
Try on traditional clothes
Listen to songs and perform simple dance moves or clapping games
Identify different traditional foods from pictures or video clips
Draw a picture of someone in traditional attire or performing a cultural dance

teacher_steps: Take learners on a short walk or use a map to identify nearby landmarks
Ask learners about special places in their town or village
Show pictures of common landmarks
Explain that landmarks are important places people visit
Play picture matching game
Ask learners to draw a landmark they know
Label classroom walls as North, South, East, West
Have learners stand and point to each direction
Show pictures of familiar landmarks
Take learners outside to relate directions to real places
Ask learners directional questions
Give learners a simple map with a school at the center
learner_steps: Identify nearby landmarks during walk or on map
Name special places in their town or village
Describe what happens at each place
Match pictures of landmarks to their names
Draw a landmark they know
Share their drawings with the class
Stand in the middle of classroom and point out North, South, East, West
Move or point in the correct direction
Relate directions to real places outside
Sing a North, South, East, West song or do movement activity
Draw and label a landmark in each direction on a simple map

sub_topics.short_notes: In this topic focuses on tourism and its impact on communities. Learners will explore the concept of tourism, how people travel to visit exciting places, and the role of tourists in supporting local economies. This topic will help learners to learn about different cultures, landmarks, and the importance of tourism in bringing people together. Cultures are ways of life of different groups of people. People have different traditions, clothing, food, language and dances that make them unique. Learning about different cultures helps us appreciate and respect others. Landmarks are important places in a community such as schools, markets, hospitals, and police stations, which learners can recognize and describe. By understanding and using the four cardinal points North, South, East, and West, learners develop the ability to locate these landmarks on simple maps or in real-life settings.

vocabulary.definition: Traveling to different places for fun, learning, or adventure
A person who travels to visit new places
The way people live, including their food, clothes, and language
Special things people do in their culture, like celebrations and festivals
A special place or building that helps people know where they are, like a school, Church, hospital, famous statue or a big tree
A way of doing things in a community, like greetings or how people eat
Important things passed down from parents and grandparents, like stories, dances, and old buildings
The four main directions used to find places on a map

criteria: Assess understanding of the diversity of cultures in their community by recognizing and naming different cultural groups
Assess ability to appreciate different cultural practices, such as clothing, food, and traditions, fostering respect for diversity
Assess application of critical thinking during group discussions
Assess ability to comprehend cultural stories and make connections with their own lives
Assess ability to showcase their memory skills by recalling specific details about cultural stories
Assess an understanding of their community by recognizing important landmarks
Assess the ability to develop geographical awareness
Assess critical thinking by categorizing landmarks based on their function
Assess the ability to locate landmarks using cardinal points

sub_topics.title: Games
competences: Creativity and innovation: Apply a set of skills that are appropriate for the creative process
Emotional Intelligence: Recognise personal emotions and that of others
Collaboration: Participate in various sports with others
Communication: Use appropriate language (verbal and non-verbal) in different situations
Critical thinking: Organise things according to their attributes, explore the environment, manipulate different objects

instructional_blocks.hook: Begin with an exciting group warm-up (marching, jumping, or clapping in a circle while singing a game song) and ask: 'Who likes playing games? Can you name one game you enjoy with your friends?'
teacher_steps: Ask learners to name games they know
Explain simple rules of selected games
Guide learners to practice games in pairs or groups
Ensure everyone participates by rotating
Reflect on how they played and followed rules
Use traditional games that are culturally relevant
Modify games to ensure safety and inclusion
learner_steps: Name games they know (tag, hide-and-seek, football, netball, Ichiyenga)
Practice games in pairs or groups
Take turns to ensure everyone participates

teacher_steps: Lead warm-up activities (jumping on spot, clapping and marching, stretching arms and legs)
Demonstrate Game 1: Traditional games - Ichiyenga
Demonstrate Game 2: Modified conventional games - Netball (Throw and Catch)
Briefly explain game and rules
Use learners to demonstrate
Modify games to suit the age group
learner_steps: Make simple movements (jumping, clapping, marching, stretching)
Learn to play Ichiyenga in small groups
Play modified netball (throw and catch)
Pass and catch ball without moving
Focus on teamwork without scoring

sub_topics.short_notes: Sports Skills Development is all about learning how to play games and take part in sports activities that help us become strong, healthy, and active. It also helps learn how to work with others, follow rules, and have fun. Games are fun activities that can be played with friends. Some are traditional games from our culture, while others are modern games like football and netball. Games being the activities or contests with rules, often undertaken for entertainment, recreation, or competition, are therefore, important in the life of a learner as they give the hands-on practical knowledge acquisition as well as skills development.

vocabulary.definition: A fun activity with rules played by one or more people
Guidelines that show how a game or sport is played
A team running race where each person takes turns to run
A race where you hop in a sack from start to finish
The place where running races happen
Activities like jumping or throwing done on the field

criteria: Assess knowledge and understanding of various games by naming familiar games
Assess ability to practice basic physical movements and improve their motor skills through game participation
Assess an understanding of game rules by following the instructions and rules of each game
Assess the ability to engage with culturally relevant traditional games

sub_topics.title: Field and Track Events
competences: Creativity and innovation: Apply a set of skills that are appropriate for the creative process
Emotional Intelligence: Recognise personal emotions and that of others
Collaboration: Participate in various sports with others
Communication: Use appropriate language (verbal and non-verbal) in different situations
Critical thinking: Organise things according to their attributes, explore the environment, manipulate different objects

teacher_steps: Show pictures/videos of long jumpers, high jumpers, or people throwing balls
Let learners identify the actions
Group learners to match flashcards of event names to pictures
Create a class chart showing track events and field events
Explain field events take place on the field
learner_steps: Identify and name Track Events (running races like relays, sprints)
Identify and name Field Events (jumping or throwing like long jump, ball throw)
Identify the actions in pictures/videos
Match flashcards of event names to pictures

teacher_steps: Explain simple rules for relay, sack race, and ball throw
Keep rules very simple and practical
learner_steps: Answer guiding questions about rules
Learn what to do before running
Learn how to keep safe when jumping

teacher_steps: Use open space like field or playground
Let learners practice in small groups
Demonstrate each event first
Demonstrate standing long jump and ball throw safely
Let learners take turns
Encourage cheering, teamwork, and taking turns
Emphasize fun and participation, not winning
learner_steps: Perform Relay Race (short distance with baton)
Perform Sack Race (20 meters)
Perform Ball Throw or Bean Bag Throw
Perform Jumping on the spot or Mini long jump
Jump from a marked spot and land in sand or on a mat
Throw soft balls to see who throws the farthest

sub_topics.short_notes: Field and track events are sports done in open spaces. These help learners to have strong bodies by running, jumping, and throwing. Field and track events are athletic competitions that take place on a running track or a field, testing athletes' speed, strength, endurance, and agility. Field activities include jumping, throwing and vaults while Track events includes sprint races, middle-distance races, hurdles and relays. Field and track events being athletic competitions that take place on a running track or a field that aims at testing athletes' speed, strength, endurance, and agility are therefore, very important in the learning environment as they equip learners with different athletic skills, and also help them develop strong muscles and bones.

criteria: Assess the ability to understanding by identify/mentioning correct activities, their categories as well as demonstrating the correct skills for each given event
Assess the ability to Participate and Involvement in all events (relay, sack race, ball throw, and jump)
Assess the ability to demonstrate skill - passing a baton in relay, sack race, throw the ball, jump on the spot or forward
